r6sGWTCMz2k.prompt: A Fourier series represents a periodic function as a sum of what?
r6sGWTCMz2k.hint: Think about the building-block waves with integer-multiple frequencies.

answerOptions.text: Sines and cosines of integer-multiple frequencies
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A Fourier series builds a periodic function from sines and cosines whose frequencies are integer multiples of a base frequency.
Power series use powers of $x$; Fourier series use waves. What oscillating functions are summed?
The frequencies are integer multiples of a fundamental, not random. What waves are used?
One wave cannot capture a general periodic shape. What collection of waves is summed?

r6sGWTCMz2k.prompt: In a Fourier series, the higher-frequency terms are called what?
r6sGWTCMz2k.hint: They are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The higher terms are harmonics, with frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental.
Residues belong to complex analysis, not Fourier harmonics. What are integer-multiple frequency terms called?
Eigenvectors are vectors, not series terms here. What name fits the higher frequency waves?
Asymptotes describe limiting lines, not series terms. What are the multiples of the fundamental called?

r6sGWTCMz2k.prompt: The visual intuition in the video draws a Fourier series as what kind of construction?
r6sGWTCMz2k.hint: Picture vectors of fixed lengths turning at steady rates.

answerOptions.text: Rotating vectors (epicycles) adding tip to tail
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Each term is a rotating vector, and summing them tip to tail traces the target curve.
One fixed arrow cannot trace a curve. What turning construction is shown?
The picture is of rotating arrows, not bars. What spinning objects are added?
The construction is dynamic and rotational. What rotating objects build the curve?

r6sGWTCMz2k.prompt: Why does representing a square wave by a Fourier series require infinitely many terms?
r6sGWTCMz2k.hint: A finite sum of smooth waves stays smooth.

answerOptions.text: Its sharp jumps cannot be matched exactly by any finite sum of smooth sinusoids
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The discontinuities of a square wave need infinitely many harmonics to approach, and finitely many always fall short.

answerOptions.text: Because sines and cosines are not periodic
answerOptions.rationale: Sines and cosines are periodic; that is why they are used. Why are infinitely many needed for a square wave?

answerOptions.text: Because the square wave is not periodic
answerOptions.rationale: A square wave is periodic. What feature of it demands infinitely many terms?

answerOptions.text: Because each term has the same frequency
answerOptions.rationale: The terms have distinct harmonic frequencies. What property of the square wave forces an infinite series?

r6sGWTCMz2k.prompt: Historically, Fourier introduced these series while studying which physical problem?
r6sGWTCMz2k.hint: The video opens with this classic application.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Fourier developed his series to analyze the flow of heat in solid bodies.
Orbital mechanics is a different lineage. What heat-related problem motivated Fourier?
Population models came from other work. Which physical flow did Fourier study?
Circuit analysis came later. What thermal problem did Fourier originally address?

spUNpyF58BY.prompt: The Fourier transform decomposes a (generally non-periodic) signal into what?
spUNpyF58BY.hint: It reports how much of each frequency is present, across a continuum.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The transform expresses a signal as a continuous distribution over all frequencies.

answerOptions.text: A finite list of integer harmonics
answerOptions.rationale: A discrete harmonic list is the Fourier series picture. What continuum does the transform produce?
Even one peak sits within a full spectrum. What continuous object does the transform give?
The output lives in frequency, not as a time polynomial. What spectrum results?

spUNpyF58BY.prompt: The video's winding intuition detects a frequency by watching what quantity?
spUNpyF58BY.hint: The wound-up graph has a center of mass that moves.

answerOptions.text: The center of mass of the wound-up signal
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. When the winding frequency matches a present frequency, the center of mass swings far from the origin, marking a peak.

answerOptions.text: The slope of the signal at the origin
answerOptions.rationale: The detector is the center of mass, not a slope. What moving quantity signals a match?
Zero crossings are not the winding detector. What center-of-mass behavior marks a frequency?

answerOptions.text: The maximum height of the signal
answerOptions.rationale: Peak height alone is not the detector. What quantity of the wound graph is tracked?

spUNpyF58BY.prompt: The Fourier transform maps a signal between which two domains?
spUNpyF58BY.hint: It trades a description in one variable for one in frequency.

answerOptions.text: The time domain and the frequency domain
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The transform converts between a time-domain signal and its frequency-domain spectrum.

answerOptions.text: The position domain and the momentum domain only in physics
answerOptions.rationale: While related in physics, the general statement is about time and frequency. Which two domains does it connect?

answerOptions.text: The real axis and the complex plane
answerOptions.rationale: That is not the transform's domain pairing. What two physical domains does it relate?
Integers are not the domains here. What time and what other domain are linked?

spUNpyF58BY.prompt: When is a Fourier series used rather than a Fourier transform?
spUNpyF58BY.hint: Series handle one class of signals, transforms the other.

answerOptions.text: For periodic signals (the transform suits non-periodic signals)
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Fourier series represent periodic signals with discrete harmonics; the transform handles non-periodic signals with a continuous spectrum.
Non-periodic signals are the transform's domain. Which class does the series serve?

answerOptions.text: For signals defined only at integers
answerOptions.rationale: That describes a different (discrete) tool. Which periodicity does the series require?
Constants are a trivial case, not the general rule. What kind of signals does the series represent?

spUNpyF58BY.prompt: In the transform's spectrum, a frequency strongly present in the signal shows up as what?
spUNpyF58BY.hint: A strong match makes the detector respond sharply.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A frequency that is strongly present produces a pronounced peak in the spectrum.
A present frequency gives a large response, not a zero. What feature marks it?
A flat spectrum would mean no dominant frequency. What localized feature appears?
The feature lives in the frequency spectrum, not in time. What does a present frequency produce there?

wmCIrpLBFds.prompt: For a function of period $2L$ written as $f(x) = \tfrac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^\infty \big(a_n\cos\tfrac{n\pi x}{L} + b_n\sin\tfrac{n\pi x}{L}\big)$, the constant $a_0$ is given by which formula?
wmCIrpLBFds.hint: The constant term measures the average; integrate $f$ over a full period.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Integrating $f$ over a full period and dividing by $L$ gives $a_0$, and $a_0/2$ is the average value.
You must divide by the interval factor $L$. What normalization belongs in front of the integral?
That formula would compute the average directly, but the convention puts $a_0/2$ as the average. What prefactor defines $a_0$ itself?
The constant term carries no cosine factor. What integrand gives $a_0$?

wmCIrpLBFds.prompt: The cosine coefficients are given by which formula?
wmCIrpLBFds.hint: Project $f$ onto the matching cosine harmonic.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Each cosine coefficient is the inner product of $f$ with $\cos(n\pi x/L)$, normalized by $L$.
That projects onto sine, giving $b_n$. Which trig function pairs with $a_n$?
The harmonic factor is missing and so is the normalization. What integrand isolates the cosine coefficient?
Coefficients come from integrals, not point evaluations. What integral defines $a_n$?

wmCIrpLBFds.prompt: The sine coefficients are given by which formula?
wmCIrpLBFds.hint: Project $f$ onto the matching sine harmonic.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Each sine coefficient is the inner product of $f$ with $\sin(n\pi x/L)$, normalized by $L$.
That projects onto cosine, giving $a_n$. Which trig function pairs with $b_n$?
The normalization for the harmonics is $1/L$, not $1/(2L)$. What prefactor is correct?
Coefficients are integrals, not derivative values. What integral defines $b_n$?

wmCIrpLBFds.prompt: If a function has period $2L$, what is its fundamental period in the Fourier series convention above?
wmCIrpLBFds.hint: The basic repeat length is the full interval from $-L$ to $L$.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The function repeats over a length of $2L$, matching the harmonics $\cos(n\pi x/L)$ and $\sin(n\pi x/L)$.
The half-length $L$ is not the full repeat length. What is the distance over which $f$ repeats?
The period need not equal $\pi$; that is only the special case $L = \pi/?$. What is the period in terms of $L$?
That is a frequency-like quantity, not a period. What length is the fundamental period?

wmCIrpLBFds.prompt: The Fourier coefficient formulas follow directly from which property of the sine and cosine basis?
wmCIrpLBFds.hint: Each coefficient is isolated by integrating against one basis function.

answerOptions.text: Orthogonality of the harmonics over a period
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Because distinct harmonics are orthogonal, integrating $f$ against one of them isolates its coefficient.
The harmonics are distinct, not equal. What relation between them isolates each coefficient?
Sines and cosines are not polynomials. What orthogonality property is used?

answerOptions.text: The function being a single sine
answerOptions.rationale: The formulas apply to general $f$, not just one sine. What basis property makes them work?

ijQaTAT3kOg.prompt: For an odd function on $[-L, L]$, which Fourier coefficients vanish?
ijQaTAT3kOg.hint: Cosine is even, so an odd function has no overlap with it.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. An odd function is orthogonal to every cosine, so only sine terms survive.
Sine matches an odd function, so the sine terms remain. Which coefficients drop out?
Every cosine coefficient vanishes for an odd function, not just $a_0$. Which whole family disappears?
Parity forces half the coefficients to zero. Which family vanishes for an odd function?

ijQaTAT3kOg.prompt: For an even function on $[-L, L]$, which Fourier coefficients vanish?
ijQaTAT3kOg.hint: Sine is odd, so an even function has no overlap with it.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. An even function is orthogonal to every sine, so only cosine terms survive.
Cosines match an even function, so they remain. Which family vanishes instead?
Every sine coefficient vanishes for an even function, not just the first. Which whole family disappears?
The constant term survives for an even function. Which family drops to zero?

ijQaTAT3kOg.prompt: The Fourier series of $f(x) = x^2$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$ contains which terms?

answerOptions.text: A constant and cosine terms only
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Since $x^2$ is even, its sine coefficients vanish, leaving the constant and cosine terms.
Sine terms vanish for an even function. What kind of terms survive for $x^2$?

answerOptions.text: Both sine and cosine terms equally
answerOptions.rationale: The odd sine terms drop out for an even function. Which terms remain?
A nonzero function has a nonzero series. Which terms does an even function keep?

ijQaTAT3kOg.prompt: The average value of $f$ over one period equals which part of its Fourier series?
ijQaTAT3kOg.hint: The oscillating terms average to zero over a full period.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Every sine and cosine averages to zero over a period, leaving $a_0/2$ as the mean.
Sinusoids average to zero, so $b_1$ does not give the mean. What constant remains?
Size of a harmonic does not set the average. What term survives averaging?
The mean is zero only if $a_0 = 0$. What term equals the average in general?

ijQaTAT3kOg.prompt: At a jump discontinuity of $f$, the Fourier series converges to what value?
ijQaTAT3kOg.hint: It splits the difference between the two one-sided limits.

answerOptions.text: The midpoint (average) of the left-hand and right-hand limits
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. At a jump the series converges to the average of the one-sided limits.
It does not favor one side. What combination of the two limits does it pick?

answerOptions.text: The larger of the two limits
answerOptions.rationale: It is not the maximum. What average value does the series take?
The value is generally nonzero. What average of the side limits results?

JaiSBThC6wM.prompt: The full Fourier series on $[-L, L]$ uses which set of basis functions?
JaiSBThC6wM.hint: Both even and odd harmonics participate.

answerOptions.text: Both cosines and sines, plus a constant
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The full series combines a constant, cosine harmonics, and sine harmonics.
Cosine-only series are the half-range cosine case. What does the full series also include?
Sine-only series are the half-range sine case. What additional terms does the full series carry?
A single constant cannot represent a general function. What harmonics are also included?

JaiSBThC6wM.prompt: To apply a Fourier series to a function defined only on $[-L, L]$, what is assumed about $f$ outside that interval?
JaiSBThC6wM.hint: The series itself is periodic, so it repeats the data.

answerOptions.text: It is extended periodically with period $2L$
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The Fourier series represents the periodic extension of $f$ with period $2L$.

answerOptions.text: It is set to zero outside the interval
answerOptions.rationale: The series repeats the data rather than zeroing it. What extension does the series build?

answerOptions.text: It grows linearly outside the interval
answerOptions.rationale: Fourier series are bounded and periodic, not linearly growing. What periodic behavior is assumed?

answerOptions.text: It is undefined outside the interval
answerOptions.rationale: The series is defined for all $x$. How does it continue $f$?

JaiSBThC6wM.prompt: A Fourier series of a piecewise smooth function is guaranteed to converge under which classical result?
JaiSBThC6wM.hint: Piecewise smoothness is the key hypothesis.

answerOptions.text: It converges to $f$ at points of continuity and to the midpoint at jumps
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The convergence theorem gives $f$ where it is continuous and the average of the side limits at jumps.
Piecewise smooth functions have convergent series. Where does it converge?

answerOptions.text: It converges only at the endpoints
answerOptions.rationale: Convergence holds throughout, not just at the ends. What does it converge to at interior points?

answerOptions.text: It converges only if $f$ is a polynomial
answerOptions.rationale: Polynomials are not required. What smoothness hypothesis guarantees convergence?

JaiSBThC6wM.prompt: The overshoot of a Fourier partial sum near a jump discontinuity is known as what?
JaiSBThC6wM.hint: It is a famous named artifact at discontinuities.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The persistent overshoot near a jump is called the Gibbs phenomenon.
Runge's phenomenon concerns polynomial interpolation, not Fourier sums. What is the Fourier overshoot called?
Resonance is a forcing effect, not the jump overshoot. What name applies to the Fourier overshoot?
Aliasing is a sampling effect. What is the named overshoot near a jump?

JaiSBThC6wM.prompt: Fourier series are useful for solving linear differential equations with which kind of forcing?
JaiSBThC6wM.hint: Periodic forcing can be broken into harmonics.

answerOptions.text: Periodic forcing, by solving for each harmonic and superposing
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A periodic forcing is expanded in harmonics, each handled separately, then superposed.
Constant forcing is a trivial single term. What broader periodic forcing do Fourier methods address?
Impulses are the Laplace or Green's-function setting. What periodic forcing suits Fourier series?
Periodic forcing is a prime use case. What forcing does the harmonic approach handle?

jDLCxErtwTs.prompt: A half-range sine series represents a function on $[0, L]$ using which extension?
jDLCxErtwTs.hint: Sines are odd, so the matching extension is odd.

answerOptions.text: The odd periodic extension, giving only sine terms
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Extending $f$ as an odd function produces a pure sine series on $[0, L]$.
Even extension yields cosines, not sines. What parity gives a sine series?
A constant extension does not produce sine harmonics. What odd extension does?
The series is built from a specific extension. Which odd extension gives sines?

jDLCxErtwTs.prompt: A half-range cosine series represents a function on $[0, L]$ using which extension?
jDLCxErtwTs.hint: Cosines are even, so the matching extension is even.

answerOptions.text: The even periodic extension, giving only cosine terms
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Extending $f$ as an even function produces a pure cosine series on $[0, L]$.
Odd extension yields sines, not cosines. What parity gives a cosine series?
A linear ramp is not how the cosine series is built. What even extension does?
The extension is specifically even. Which extension yields cosine terms?

jDLCxErtwTs.prompt: A sine series is the natural choice for a heat problem with which boundary conditions?

answerOptions.text: Fixed (zero) temperature at both ends, $u(0) = u(L) = 0$
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Sine harmonics vanish at both ends, matching fixed-temperature (Dirichlet) conditions.
Insulated ends suit cosines, whose slope vanishes. What conditions do sines satisfy?
The choice is driven by the boundary conditions. Which conditions do sines match?

answerOptions.text: A fixed slope at both ends
answerOptions.rationale: Sines match fixed values, not fixed slopes. What boundary values do they satisfy?

jDLCxErtwTs.prompt: A cosine series is the natural choice for a problem with which boundary conditions?
jDLCxErtwTs.hint: Cosine has zero slope at both ends.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Cosine harmonics have vanishing derivative at both ends, matching insulated (Neumann) conditions.

answerOptions.text: Fixed zero temperature at both ends
answerOptions.rationale: Fixed values suit sines, whose value vanishes. What slope condition do cosines satisfy?

answerOptions.text: A fixed nonzero value at one end only
answerOptions.rationale: Cosine series handle symmetric slope conditions. Which derivative conditions do they match?
Half-range cosine series target Neumann conditions. Which slope conditions do they satisfy?

jDLCxErtwTs.prompt: The coefficients of a half-range sine series are given by which formula?
jDLCxErtwTs.hint: Project onto $\sin(n\pi x/L)$ over $[0, L]$ with the half-range normalization.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The half-range sine coefficient integrates over $[0, L]$ with the factor $2/L$.
The half-range convention uses $2/L$, not $1/L$. What prefactor is correct?
Sine coefficients pair with sine, not cosine. Which trig function belongs in the integrand?
The half-range integral runs over $[0, L]$, not $[-L, L]$. What limits are correct?

dIsxevQhzic.prompt: The method of separation of variables seeks a solution of the form what?
dIsxevQhzic.hint: Split the dependence on each variable into its own factor.

answerOptions.text: $u(x, t) = X(x)\,T(t)$, a product of single-variable functions
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Separation of variables assumes the solution factors into a function of $x$ times a function of $t$.
Separation uses a product, not a sum. How are the single-variable factors combined?
One factor must depend on $t$. Which variables do the two factors use?
Each factor should carry its own variable, $X$ in $x$ and $T$ in $t$. What is the correct product form?

dIsxevQhzic.prompt: After substituting $u = X(x)T(t)$ and dividing, each side of the equation depends on a single variable. What does this force?
dIsxevQhzic.hint: If a function of $x$ equals a function of $t$ for all $x$ and $t$, both must be constant.

answerOptions.text: Both sides equal a common constant (the separation constant)
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A function of $x$ alone equal to a function of $t$ alone must be constant, the separation constant.
They equal a shared constant, not necessarily zero. What common value must they take?
The point is that the sides are independent yet equal. What must each side then be?
Separation succeeds precisely here. What common quantity do the two sides share?

dIsxevQhzic.prompt: Separation of variables turns one partial differential equation into what?
dIsxevQhzic.hint: Each single-variable factor obeys its own equation.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The separation produces an ODE for $X(x)$ and an ODE for $T(t)$.
Separation reduces, not enlarges, the problem. What simpler equations result?
Derivatives remain, so the results are differential. How many ODEs appear?
No integral equation is produced. What kind of equations does separation yield?

dIsxevQhzic.prompt: In the separated heat equation, the spatial factor $X(x)$ together with the boundary conditions forms what?
dIsxevQhzic.hint: The constant ties $X$ to an eigenvalue problem.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The spatial ODE plus boundary conditions is exactly an eigenvalue problem whose eigenvalues are the allowed separation constants.
The spatial part carries boundary conditions, not initial data. What kind of problem does $X$ solve?
Derivatives in $x$ remain. What boundary-condition problem does $X$ satisfy?
The separated spatial equation is linear. What linear eigenvalue problem results?

dIsxevQhzic.prompt: The role of the separation constant in the spatial equation is to play the part of what?
dIsxevQhzic.hint: It is the value that the boundary conditions quantize.

answerOptions.text: An eigenvalue selected by the boundary conditions
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The boundary conditions admit only discrete separation constants, the eigenvalues of the spatial problem.
It is constrained, not free; the boundary conditions pin it. What does it become?
The constant is not the initial data. What spectral quantity does it represent?
It is not a speed. What eigenvalue role does the separation constant take?

17WkgjkENV0.prompt: The one-dimensional heat equation is which partial differential equation?
17WkgjkENV0.hint: It is first order in time and second order in space.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The heat equation relates the first time derivative to the second spatial derivative through the diffusivity $\alpha$.
That second time derivative is the wave equation. What single time derivative defines the heat equation?
That is Laplace's equation, with no time at all. What time-dependent equation models heat?
Diffusion involves the second spatial derivative, not the first. Which spatial derivative appears in the heat equation?

17WkgjkENV0.prompt: The one-dimensional wave equation is which partial differential equation?
17WkgjkENV0.hint: It is second order in both time and space.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The wave equation sets the second time derivative equal to $c^2$ times the second spatial derivative.
A single time derivative gives the heat equation. What time derivative does the wave equation use?
That first-order transport equation is not the wave equation. What second-order form is correct?
That steady-state Laplace equation has no time. What time-dependent equation describes waves?

17WkgjkENV0.prompt: Laplace's equation in two dimensions is which equation?
17WkgjkENV0.hint: It is the steady-state equation with no time dependence.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Laplace's equation sets the sum of the second spatial derivatives to zero, describing steady states.
That includes time and is a heat (diffusion) equation. What steady equation drops the time term?
The signs should both be positive in Laplace's equation. What is the correct combination?
That is a wave equation in two space dimensions. What time-free equation is Laplace's?

17WkgjkENV0.prompt: Among heat, wave, and Laplace equations, which describes a steady state with no time dependence?
17WkgjkENV0.hint: Steady state means nothing changes in time.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Laplace's equation has no time variable and models steady-state (equilibrium) configurations.
The heat equation evolves in time toward steady state. Which equation has no time at all?
The wave equation oscillates in time. Which equation is purely steady?
One of the three is time independent. Which one models the steady state?

17WkgjkENV0.prompt: After finding many separated solutions, how is the initial condition generally satisfied?
17WkgjkENV0.hint: Linear equations let solutions be added, with coefficients from a Fourier expansion.

answerOptions.text: By superposing the separated modes and choosing coefficients via a Fourier expansion
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Summing the eigenmodes and matching the initial data through a Fourier series fixes the coefficients.

answerOptions.text: By keeping only the first mode
answerOptions.rationale: A single mode rarely matches arbitrary initial data. What combination of modes is used?

answerOptions.text: By multiplying all the modes together
answerOptions.rationale: Linear superposition adds modes, it does not multiply them. How are they combined?
The initial condition must be met. What expansion determines the coefficients?

EW08rD-GFh0.prompt: In two dimensions, the Laplacian of $u$ is which expression?
EW08rD-GFh0.hint: It is the divergence of the gradient, summing the pure second partials.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The Laplacian sums the unmixed second partial derivatives.
First derivatives form the gradient, not the Laplacian. What second derivatives are summed?
The mixed partial is not the Laplacian. Which pure second partials are added?
It is a sum, not a product. What combination of second partials defines it?

EW08rD-GFh0.prompt: Intuitively, the Laplacian at a point measures what?
EW08rD-GFh0.hint: It compares the value at the point with the surrounding average.

answerOptions.text: How the value at a point compares with the average of its neighbors
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The Laplacian captures the difference between a point's value and the average of nearby values.

answerOptions.text: The slope of $u$ in the $x$ direction
answerOptions.rationale: A single slope is a first derivative. What neighborhood comparison does the Laplacian make?
It is a comparison with the average, not the maximum. What average does it use?
It is a local, not global, quantity. What local comparison does it express?

EW08rD-GFh0.prompt: If $\nabla^2 u > 0$ at a point, the value there is generally what relative to its neighbors?
EW08rD-GFh0.hint: A positive Laplacian signals a local dip.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A positive Laplacian means the point sits below the average of its neighbors.
That corresponds to a negative Laplacian. What does a positive value indicate?
Equality corresponds to a zero Laplacian. What does a positive sign indicate?
A positive Laplacian suggests a dip, not a peak. How does the point compare with neighbors?

EW08rD-GFh0.prompt: A function satisfying $\nabla^2 u = 0$ is called what?
EW08rD-GFh0.hint: This is the steady-state Laplace condition.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Functions with zero Laplacian are called harmonic functions.
Zero Laplacian does not mean periodic. What name describes $\nabla^2 u = 0$?
Harmonic functions are typically smooth, not singular. What is the term for zero Laplacian?
Harmonic functions need not be linear. What special name applies when the Laplacian vanishes?

EW08rD-GFh0.prompt: The Laplacian appears as the spatial operator in which of these equations?
EW08rD-GFh0.hint: It is the common spatial term across the classical PDEs.

answerOptions.text: The heat, wave, and Laplace equations
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The Laplacian is the shared spatial operator in the heat, wave, and Laplace equations.
It appears in more than just the heat equation. Which family of equations shares it?
Those use a first derivative, not the Laplacian. Which second-order equations use it?
The Laplacian is central to them. Which classical equations contain it?

yK-xFXATzyQ.prompt: The one-dimensional heat equation is which of these?
yK-xFXATzyQ.hint: First order in time, second order in space, with a diffusivity constant.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The heat equation links the first time derivative to the second spatial derivative through the constant $k$.
Two time derivatives give the wave equation. What single time derivative defines heat flow?
Diffusion uses the second spatial derivative. Which spatial derivative belongs in the heat equation?
The roles of time and space are reversed here. What is the standard heat equation?

yK-xFXATzyQ.prompt: For a rod with ends held at zero temperature, the separated solution of the heat equation is which series?
yK-xFXATzyQ.hint: Sine modes in space, decaying exponentials in time.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Each sine mode decays in time with rate $k(n\pi/L)^2$, summed against the initial data.
A growing exponential is unphysical for cooling. What sign should the time exponent have?
Zero-temperature ends call for sines, which vanish at the ends. Which spatial function fits?
Oscillating time factors belong to the wave equation. What time behavior does heat flow show?

yK-xFXATzyQ.prompt: In the heat-equation solution, which modes decay fastest in time?
yK-xFXATzyQ.hint: The decay rate grows with the square of the mode number.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Since the decay rate is $k(n\pi/L)^2$, larger $n$ decays faster, smoothing the profile.
Small $n$ decays slowest, not fastest. Which modes have the largest decay rate?

answerOptions.text: All modes decay at the same rate
answerOptions.rationale: The rate depends on $n^2$, so it varies. Which modes decay quickest?
A constant mode would not decay at all. Which high-$n$ modes vanish soonest?

yK-xFXATzyQ.prompt: As $t \to \infty$ for a rod with zero-temperature ends, the heat-equation solution approaches what?

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Each mode decays to zero, so the temperature approaches zero throughout the rod.
With both ends at zero, no nonzero steady value survives. What limit do the decaying modes give?
The modes decay, they do not grow. What value is approached as $t$ increases?
Heat flow decays rather than oscillates. What steady limit results?

yK-xFXATzyQ.prompt: How are the coefficients $b_n$ in the heat-equation solution determined?
yK-xFXATzyQ.hint: Match the solution at $t = 0$ to the initial temperature.

answerOptions.text: By expanding the initial temperature distribution in a sine series
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Setting $t = 0$ leaves a sine series whose coefficients are the Fourier sine coefficients of the initial data.
The boundary conditions fix the mode shapes, not the coefficients. What initial data determines $b_n$?
The constant $k$ sets decay rates, not the coefficients. What expansion of the initial profile gives $b_n$?

answerOptions.text: They are all equal to one
answerOptions.rationale: The coefficients depend on the initial profile. What sine expansion produces them?

6lbSrBmGUik.prompt: The one-dimensional wave equation is which of these?
6lbSrBmGUik.hint: It is second order in time, unlike the heat equation.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The wave equation sets the second time derivative equal to $c^2$ times the second spatial derivative.
A single time derivative gives the heat equation. What time derivative does the wave equation use?
The spatial term is a second derivative scaled by $c^2$. What is the correct right side?
Here $c$ is a wave speed, and the standard form solves for $u_{tt}$. What is the usual arrangement?

6lbSrBmGUik.prompt: For a string fixed at both ends, the time factors of the separated wave solution are which functions?
6lbSrBmGUik.hint: With no decay, the time behavior oscillates.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The wave equation gives oscillatory time factors at the modal frequencies, with no decay.
Exponential decay belongs to the heat equation. What oscillatory time factors arise for waves?
Wave modes oscillate rather than grow. What bounded time functions appear?
The modes vibrate in time. What oscillating functions describe them?

6lbSrBmGUik.prompt: In the wave equation $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$, the constant $c$ represents what?
6lbSrBmGUik.hint: It carries units of distance over time.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The constant $c$ is the speed at which disturbances travel along the medium.
Diffusivity belongs to the heat equation. What does $c$ measure for waves?
Amplitude is set by initial data, not by $c$. What rate does $c$ represent?
The period depends on $c$ and the mode, but $c$ itself is a speed. What quantity is $c$?

6lbSrBmGUik.prompt: How many initial conditions does the wave equation require, and why?
6lbSrBmGUik.hint: It is second order in time.

answerOptions.text: Two: initial position and initial velocity, since it is second order in time
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Being second order in time, the wave equation needs both the initial displacement and the initial velocity.
A single condition underdetermines a second-order-in-time equation. What second piece of data is needed?
Initial data in time is still required. How many time conditions does second order need?
Acceleration is set by the equation itself. How many independent initial conditions are required?

6lbSrBmGUik.prompt: D'Alembert's solution of the wave equation expresses $u$ as what?
6lbSrBmGUik.hint: Two shapes travel in opposite directions at speed $c$.

answerOptions.text: A sum of left- and right-traveling waves, $f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. D'Alembert's form is the superposition of a right-moving wave $f(x - ct)$ and a left-moving wave $g(x + ct)$.

answerOptions.text: A single decaying exponential in time
answerOptions.rationale: Decay describes diffusion, not waves. What traveling-wave combination solves it?
That separated product is one family of solutions, but d'Alembert's form is different. What traveling waves appear?

answerOptions.text: A constant plus a linear term
answerOptions.rationale: That cannot capture propagation. What pair of traveling waves does d'Alembert use?

BNULJwNPmFk.prompt: Duhamel's principle is a technique for solving which kind of wave equation?
BNULJwNPmFk.hint: It addresses an external driving term on the right-hand side.

answerOptions.text: The forced (inhomogeneous) wave equation with a source term
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Duhamel's principle constructs the response of a forced wave equation from its impulse responses.
The homogeneous case needs no source handling. What kind of equation does Duhamel target?
Laplace's equation has no time forcing. Which time-dependent forced equation does Duhamel address?
Duhamel applies to the second-order forced wave equation. What source-driven equation is it for?

BNULJwNPmFk.prompt: The central idea of Duhamel's principle is to build the forced response by doing what?
BNULJwNPmFk.hint: Treat the forcing as a continuous train of impulses and add their effects.

answerOptions.text: Superposing the responses to impulses applied at each earlier time
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The forcing is decomposed into impulses, and their individual responses are integrated over time.
Duhamel integrates impulse responses rather than differentiating the source. How are the impulses combined?
The forcing is exactly what is being handled. How does Duhamel incorporate it?

answerOptions.text: Replacing the forcing with a constant
answerOptions.rationale: The method respects the full time-varying source. How does it accumulate the source's effect?

BNULJwNPmFk.prompt: The full solution of a forced wave equation is written as which combination?
BNULJwNPmFk.hint: Add the free response to the response due to the source.

answerOptions.text: A homogeneous solution plus a particular (forced) solution
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. As with linear ODEs, the total is the homogeneous solution plus a particular solution driven by the forcing.
The homogeneous part carries the initial data. What is added to the particular solution?
The forcing must contribute a particular part. What is added to the homogeneous solution?

answerOptions.text: The product of homogeneous and particular solutions
answerOptions.rationale: Linear superposition adds the two, it does not multiply. How are they combined?

BNULJwNPmFk.prompt: In the forced wave equation $u_{tt} - c^2 u_{xx} = f(x, t)$, the term $f(x, t)$ represents what?
BNULJwNPmFk.hint: It is the term that makes the equation inhomogeneous.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The function $f(x, t)$ is the external forcing driving the wave equation.
The speed is the constant $c$, not $f$. What does the right-hand term represent?
Initial displacement is initial data, not the right-hand side. What is $f(x, t)$?
Boundary conditions are imposed separately. What role does $f(x, t)$ play in the equation?

BNULJwNPmFk.prompt: Duhamel's principle relies on which structural property of the wave equation?
BNULJwNPmFk.hint: Responses to separate inputs can be added.

answerOptions.text: Linearity, so responses to separate forcings superpose
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Linearity is what lets the responses to individual impulses be summed into the total response.
The wave equation here is linear, and that is essential. What property allows superposing impulse responses?
Boundary conditions can be present. What structural property underlies Duhamel's method?
Duhamel handles time-varying forcing precisely because of one property. Which property is it?

unitMastery.prompt: A Fourier series represents a periodic function as a sum of what?
unitMastery.hint: Think of the integer-multiple-frequency building-block waves.

answerOptions.text: Sines and cosines of integer-multiple frequencies
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The series sums harmonics whose frequencies are integer multiples of a fundamental.
Powers belong to a power series. What oscillating functions does Fourier use?
One wave cannot capture a general shape. What collection of waves is summed?
The frequencies are integer multiples, not random. What waves are used?

unitMastery.prompt: Why does a square wave require infinitely many Fourier terms?
unitMastery.hint: Smooth finite sums stay smooth.

answerOptions.text: Its sharp jumps cannot be matched exactly by any finite sum of smooth sinusoids
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Discontinuities require infinitely many harmonics to approach.
A square wave is periodic. What feature demands an infinite series?
Sines are periodic. What property of the square wave forces infinitely many terms?

answerOptions.text: Because each term has the same frequency
answerOptions.rationale: The harmonics have distinct frequencies. What jump feature forces an infinite series?

unitMastery.prompt: The Fourier transform decomposes a non-periodic signal into what?
unitMastery.hint: It reports a continuum of frequency content.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The transform gives a continuous distribution over frequency.
A discrete harmonic list is the series picture. What continuum does the transform give?
Even a dominant frequency sits in a full spectrum. What continuous object results?
The output is in frequency, not a time polynomial. What spectrum results?

unitMastery.prompt: When is a Fourier series used instead of a Fourier transform?
unitMastery.hint: Series handle one periodicity class, transforms the other.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Series represent periodic signals; the transform suits non-periodic ones.
Those are the transform's domain. Which class does the series serve?
Constants are a trivial case. What periodicity does the series require?

answerOptions.text: For signals defined only at integers
answerOptions.rationale: That is a discrete tool. What kind of signals does the series represent?

unitMastery.prompt: In a Fourier transform spectrum, a strongly present frequency appears as what?
unitMastery.hint: A strong match makes the detector respond sharply.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A present frequency produces a pronounced spectral peak.
A present frequency gives a large response. What feature marks it?
Flatness means no dominant frequency. What localized feature appears?
The feature lives in frequency. What does a present frequency produce there?

unitMastery.prompt: For a function of period $2L$, the cosine coefficient $a_n$ is given by which formula?
unitMastery.hint: Project $f$ onto the matching cosine, normalized by $L$.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Each cosine coefficient is the inner product of $f$ with $\cos(n\pi x/L)$, normalized by $L$.
That projects onto sine, giving $b_n$. Which trig function pairs with $a_n$?
The harmonic factor and normalization are missing. What integrand gives $a_n$?
Coefficients are integrals, not point values. What integral defines $a_n$?

unitMastery.prompt: The average value of a function over one period equals which part of its Fourier series?
unitMastery.hint: Sinusoids average to zero over a period.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. All harmonics average to zero, leaving $a_0/2$ as the mean.
Sinusoids average to zero. What constant term survives?
Size does not set the average. What term remains after averaging?
The mean is zero only if $a_0 = 0$. What term equals the average in general?

unitMastery.prompt: For an odd function on $[-L, L]$, which Fourier coefficients vanish?
unitMastery.hint: Cosine is even; an odd function has no overlap with it.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. An odd function is orthogonal to every cosine, leaving only sine terms.
Sine matches an odd function, so they survive. Which family vanishes?
Every cosine coefficient vanishes, not just $a_0$. Which whole family disappears?
Parity forces half to vanish. Which family goes to zero for an odd function?

unitMastery.prompt: The Fourier series of $f(x) = x^2$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$ contains which terms?
unitMastery.hint: Determine the parity of $x^2$.

answerOptions.text: A constant and cosine terms only
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Since $x^2$ is even, the sine terms vanish.
Sines vanish for an even function. What terms survive?
The odd sine terms drop out. Which terms remain?
A nonzero function has a nonzero series. Which terms does an even function keep?

unitMastery.prompt: At a jump discontinuity, the Fourier series converges to which value?
unitMastery.hint: It splits the difference between the one-sided limits.

answerOptions.text: The midpoint of the left and right limits
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The series converges to the average of the one-sided limits at a jump.
It does not favor one side. What average does it take?
It is not the maximum. What average value results?
The value is generally nonzero. What average of the side limits does it pick?

unitMastery.prompt: A half-range sine series represents a function on $[0, L]$ using which extension?
unitMastery.hint: Sines are odd.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. An odd extension produces a pure sine series.
Even extension gives cosines. What parity yields sines?
That does not produce sine harmonics. What odd extension does?
A specific extension builds the series. Which odd one gives sines?

unitMastery.prompt: A sine series suits a heat problem with which boundary conditions?
unitMastery.hint: Sine vanishes at both ends.

answerOptions.text: Fixed zero temperature at both ends
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Sines vanish at the ends, matching Dirichlet conditions.
Insulated ends suit cosines. What conditions do sines match?
The choice depends on the conditions. Which do sines satisfy?

answerOptions.text: A fixed slope at both ends
answerOptions.rationale: Sines match fixed values, not slopes. What conditions do they satisfy?

unitMastery.prompt: A cosine series suits a problem with which boundary conditions?
unitMastery.hint: Cosine has zero slope at both ends.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Cosines have vanishing derivative at the ends, matching Neumann conditions.

answerOptions.text: Fixed zero temperature at both ends
answerOptions.rationale: Fixed values suit sines. What slope conditions do cosines match?

answerOptions.text: A fixed value at one end only
answerOptions.rationale: Cosine series handle symmetric slope conditions. Which derivatives vanish?
Half-range cosine series target Neumann conditions. Which conditions do they satisfy?

unitMastery.prompt: The overshoot of a Fourier partial sum near a jump is called what?
unitMastery.hint: It is a famous named artifact.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The persistent overshoot near a jump is the Gibbs phenomenon.
That concerns polynomial interpolation. What is the Fourier overshoot called?
Resonance is a forcing effect. What name fits the jump overshoot?
Aliasing is a sampling effect. What is the named overshoot near a jump?

unitMastery.prompt: The half-range sine coefficients are given by which formula?
unitMastery.hint: Project onto $\sin(n\pi x/L)$ over $[0, L]$ with factor $2/L$.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The half-range sine coefficient integrates over $[0, L]$ with the factor $2/L$.
The half-range convention uses $2/L$. What prefactor is correct?
Sine coefficients pair with sine. Which function belongs in the integrand?
The half-range integral runs over $[0, L]$. What limits are correct?

unitMastery.prompt: Separation of variables seeks a solution of which form?
unitMastery.hint: Split the dependence on each variable into its own factor.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The solution is assumed to factor into a function of $x$ times a function of $t$.
Separation uses a product. How are the factors combined?
One factor must depend on $t$. Which variables do the factors use?
Each factor should carry its own variable. What is the correct product?

unitMastery.prompt: After substituting $u = X T$ and dividing, each side depends on one variable. What follows?
unitMastery.hint: A function of $x$ equal to a function of $t$ for all values must be constant.

answerOptions.text: Both sides equal a common separation constant
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Independence forces both sides to equal a shared constant.
They share a constant, not necessarily zero. What common value do they take?
They stay independent yet equal. What must each side be?
Separation succeeds here. What shared quantity appears?

unitMastery.prompt: Separation of variables turns one PDE into what?
unitMastery.hint: Each single-variable factor obeys its own equation.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. One ODE for $X(x)$ and one for $T(t)$ result.
Separation reduces the problem. What simpler equations result?
Derivatives remain, so they are differential. How many ODEs appear?
No integral equation arises. What kind of equations result?

unitMastery.prompt: In the separated heat equation, the spatial part $X(x)$ with its boundary conditions forms what?
unitMastery.hint: The separation constant becomes a quantized eigenvalue.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The spatial ODE plus boundary conditions is an eigenvalue problem selecting the separation constants.
The spatial part carries boundary conditions, not initial data. What problem does $X$ solve?
Spatial derivatives remain. What boundary-condition problem does $X$ satisfy?
The separated spatial equation is linear. What linear eigenvalue problem results?

unitMastery.prompt: After finding many separated modes, how is an arbitrary initial condition matched?
unitMastery.hint: Linearity allows adding modes with Fourier coefficients.

answerOptions.text: By superposing the modes and choosing coefficients via a Fourier expansion
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Summing eigenmodes and matching the initial data through a Fourier series fixes the coefficients.

answerOptions.text: By keeping only the first mode
answerOptions.rationale: A single mode rarely matches arbitrary data. What combination is used?
Superposition adds modes. How are they combined?
The initial condition must be met. What expansion sets the coefficients?

unitMastery.prompt: In two dimensions, the Laplacian of $u$ is which expression?
unitMastery.hint: It sums the unmixed second partial derivatives.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The Laplacian sums the pure second partials.
First derivatives form the gradient. What second derivatives are summed?
The mixed partial is not the Laplacian. Which pure second partials add?
It is a sum, not a product. What combination defines it?

unitMastery.prompt: The Laplacian at a point measures what intuitively?
unitMastery.hint: It compares a point with the average of its neighbors.

answerOptions.text: How the value compares with the average of nearby values
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The Laplacian captures the difference between a point and its neighborhood average.
A slope is a first derivative. What neighborhood comparison does it make?
It compares with the average, not the maximum. What average does it use?
It is local, not global. What local comparison does it express?

unitMastery.prompt: A function satisfying $\nabla^2 u = 0$ is called what?
unitMastery.hint: This is the steady-state Laplace condition.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Functions with zero Laplacian are harmonic.
Zero Laplacian does not mean periodic. What term applies?
Harmonic functions are typically smooth. What is the term for zero Laplacian?
Harmonic functions need not be linear. What special name applies?

unitMastery.prompt: If $\nabla^2 u > 0$ at a point, the value there is generally what relative to neighbors?
unitMastery.hint: A positive Laplacian signals a local dip.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A positive Laplacian means the point sits below its neighborhood average.
That is a negative Laplacian. What does a positive value indicate?
Equality is a zero Laplacian. What does a positive sign indicate?
A positive value suggests a dip, not a peak. How does it compare with neighbors?

unitMastery.prompt: The Laplacian is the shared spatial operator in which equations?
unitMastery.hint: It is common to the classical second-order PDEs.

answerOptions.text: The heat, wave, and Laplace equations
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. All three classical PDEs use the Laplacian as their spatial operator.
It appears in more than the heat equation. Which family shares it?
Those use a first derivative. Which second-order equations use it?
It is central to them. Which classical equations contain it?

unitMastery.prompt: The one-dimensional heat equation is which of these?
unitMastery.hint: First order in time, second in space.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The heat equation links the first time derivative to the second spatial derivative.
Two time derivatives give the wave equation. What single time derivative defines heat?
That is Laplace's equation. What time-dependent equation models heat?
Diffusion uses the second spatial derivative. Which derivative belongs in the heat equation?

unitMastery.prompt: The one-dimensional wave equation is which of these?
unitMastery.hint: Second order in both time and space.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The wave equation equates the second time derivative to $c^2$ times the second spatial derivative.
A single time derivative gives the heat equation. What time derivative does the wave equation use?
That first-order transport equation is not the wave equation. What second-order form is correct?
That steady Laplace equation has no time. What time-dependent equation describes waves?

unitMastery.prompt: For a heated rod with zero-temperature ends, the separated solution has which time behavior?
unitMastery.hint: The time factor decays for diffusion.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Each mode decays exponentially at a rate set by $k(n\pi/L)^2$.
Oscillation belongs to the wave equation. What time behavior does diffusion show?
Cooling does not grow. What sign does the time exponent carry?
The modes change in time. What decaying behavior occurs?

unitMastery.prompt: How many initial conditions does the wave equation need, and why?
unitMastery.hint: It is second order in time.

answerOptions.text: Two: initial position and initial velocity, since it is second order in time
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Being second order in time, it needs both initial displacement and initial velocity.
That underdetermines second order. What second datum is needed?
Initial data in time is still required. How many time conditions are needed?
Acceleration is set by the equation. How many independent initial conditions are required?

unitMastery.prompt: D'Alembert's solution of the wave equation expresses $u$ as what?
unitMastery.hint: Two shapes travel in opposite directions at speed $c$.

answerOptions.text: A sum of traveling waves, $f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. It superposes a right-moving wave $f(x - ct)$ and a left-moving wave $g(x + ct)$.
Decay describes diffusion, not waves. What traveling-wave combination solves it?
That separated form is different from d'Alembert's. What traveling waves appear?

answerOptions.text: A constant plus a linear term
answerOptions.rationale: That cannot capture propagation. What traveling waves does d'Alembert use?
